name: Wyoming
tagline: Cheap, abundant power plus the nation's friendliest digital-asset law are turning Wyoming into a magnet for hyperscale AI data centers — even as the state runs IT on one of the leanest budgets in the country.

execSummary.summary: Wyoming is a small-budget state with an outsized AI-infrastructure story. It is the fourth-largest net energy producer in the U.S. and uses a fraction of what it generates, so Governor Gordon's 'Winning the AI Race' push is converting cheap, surplus power into a hyperscale data-center boom around Cheyenne — Meta's $800M+ campus, Microsoft's existing site plus a planned 3,200-acre expansion, and the Crusoe/Tallgrass 'Project Jade' campus starting at 1.8GW with room to scale to 10GW. The state also leads the nation on digital-asset law (45+ blockchain bills since 2016, SPDI banks, DAO LLCs, and the now-live Frontier Stable Token, FRNT). For Dell, the state's own IT spend is modest and runs through Enterprise Technology Services (ETS) under A&I via NASPO ValuePoint and statewide contracts — but the data-center buildout, the University of Wyoming, and the NCAR-Wyoming Supercomputing Center are where the real AI-infrastructure and HPC opportunity sits. ETS's 2026-2028 plan is centered on governance and cybersecurity hardening rather than big new programs, so security and modernization remain the central-government angles.
opportunities: AI-infrastructure / 'AI factory' demand riding the Cheyenne data-center boom (Crusoe Project Jade, Meta, Microsoft) — PowerEdge, networking, services, and ecosystem pull-through.
University of Wyoming and the NCAR-Wyoming Supercomputing Center (NWSC) as established HPC/research-computing buyers with GPU refresh cycles.
Cybersecurity and risk-management modernization — the explicit top theme of the ETS 2026-2028 strategic plan for a lean central IT org.
Endpoint, client, and lifecycle refresh across executive-branch agencies on a tight biennial budget where value and consolidation matter.

stats.label: Blockchain/digital-asset laws
stats.note: Enacted since 2016; FRNT stable token now live

budget.intro: Wyoming budgets on a two-year (biennial) cycle, with the main budget set in even-year general sessions. The 2025-26 biennial budget totals roughly $10.9B all-funds (about $3.4B general fund). There is no single consolidated 'state IT budget'; technology spend is distributed across agencies, and Enterprise Technology Services (ETS) sets rate structures that agencies use to build their IT ('400-series') budgets each biennium. Wyoming pursues digital transformation on a fraction of the budget larger states have — a structural reason value, consolidation, and shared services resonate. The 2025 supplemental budget did not pass, leaving some agencies strained heading into the 2027-28 cycle; Governor Gordon has since proposed an ~$11.13B 2027-28 budget.
notes: Figures are biennial (two-year) totals; Wyoming has no single line-item 'state IT budget.'
ETS funds much of its delivery through an internal-service rate model that agencies pay into — confirm where a given workload's dollars actually sit before forecasting.

initiatives.name: Cheyenne AI Data-Center Boom
initiatives.summary: Cheap, surplus power is drawing hyperscale AI capacity to the Cheyenne area: Meta's $800M+ campus, Microsoft's existing datacenters plus a planned 3,200-acre expansion (announced April 2026) and continued 'Project Cosmo' activity, and the Crusoe/Tallgrass 'Project Jade' campus (scaled to 2.7GW by Jan 2026, designed toward 10GW). Note: Crusoe PAUSED Project Jade in June 2026 'at the request of our customer,' so the state's flagship multi-GW campus is on hold even as Microsoft/Meta buildout continues. Part of Gordon's 'Winning the AI Race' agenda — still the strongest tailwind for Dell AI-infrastructure and ISG demand, with near-term uncertainty on Jade.

initiatives.name: Winning the AI Race (Gordon agenda)
initiatives.summary: Governor Gordon is positioning Wyoming to convert exported energy into in-state value via AI infrastructure, courting Google, Microsoft, Meta and AWS (a closed-door Jackson summit in March 2026) while insisting developers 'do it the Wyoming way' on power, water, and community impact. The framing — digital sovereignty built on Wyoming power — aligns well with sovereign/private AI infrastructure positioning.

initiatives.name: Wyoming Stable Token (FRNT) & Digital-Asset Leadership
initiatives.summary: Wyoming has enacted 45+ digital-asset laws since 2016 (SPDI banks, DAO LLCs) and publicly launched the Frontier Stable Token (FRNT, branded WYST during development) in Jan 2026 — the first fiat-backed, fully-reserved stable token issued by a U.S. public entity, with the commission chaired by Governor Gordon. The brand makes Wyoming a national destination for blockchain/fintech compute and signals an innovation-forward government — relevant context more than a direct hardware play, but it reinforces the digital-sovereignty narrative.

initiatives.name: NCAR-Wyoming Supercomputing Center (NWSC)
initiatives.summary: The NWSC in Cheyenne — an NCAR / University of Wyoming collaboration — runs the GPU-accelerated 'Derecho' system (19.87 petaflops, 382 NVIDIA A100 GPUs) for climate, wildfire, and space-weather research. An established HPC buyer with a refresh cadence. Note risk: NSF moved in Feb 2026 to transition NWSC operations (proposing a transfer toward the University of Wyoming), but UCAR sued and a federal court issued a March 2026 injunction blocking the transition — status quo (UCAR-managed) holds for now. Qualify governance and funding before forecasting.

initiatives.name: ETS Cybersecurity & Governance Modernization
initiatives.summary: The ETS 2026-2028 strategic plan centers on IT governance maturation, foundational service improvement, financial stewardship, and 'enhancing cybersecurity and risk management' (asset hygiene, identity/access, incident response) rather than large new programs. For a lean central IT org, the opening is security, consolidation, and lifecycle modernization that map to these themes.

procurement.intro: Wyoming's central IT organization is Enterprise Technology Services (ETS), with statewide purchasing run through the A&I (Administration & Information) General Services / Procurement Section. State agencies commonly buy IT off NASPO ValuePoint cooperative contracts (which satisfy Wyoming's competitive-bid statute) and A&I 'all-agency' statewide contracts. ETS also runs its own IT network-bid process for circuits/bandwidth. There is no single DIR-style co-op; the motion is leaner and contract-driven.

vehicles.name: NASPO ValuePoint
vehicles.detail: Primary cooperative path. Wyoming participating addenda (e.g., cloud solutions, IT software/hardware) satisfy competitive-bid requirements — confirm Dell/reseller coverage and current pricing.

vehicles.name: A&I Statewide / All-Agency Contracts
vehicles.detail: Pre-competed statewide contracts agencies can buy from without running their own solicitation; managed by A&I General Services Purchasing.

vehicles.name: ETS IT Network Bids
vehicles.detail: ETS-run competitive process for new/upgraded network circuits and bandwidth across the state.

entities.name: Enterprise Technology Services (ETS)
entities.role: Central executive-branch IT: governance, foundational services, cybersecurity, and the agency rate model — owns statewide tech direction.

entities.name: A&I General Services — Purchasing
entities.role: Statewide procurement authority; runs solicitations and all-agency contracts.

entities.name: University of Wyoming / NWSC
entities.role: Independent research-computing and HPC buyer (Derecho); GPU and infrastructure refresh.

entities.name: Hyperscale data-center operators (Crusoe, Meta, Microsoft)
entities.role: Private buyers driving the Cheyenne AI buildout — ecosystem and infrastructure pull-through.

procurement.competition: Wyoming is a lean, contract-driven market: most large IT buys flow through NASPO ValuePoint and A&I statewide contracts where incumbents already hold coverage. With a tight central budget, differentiation comes from value, consolidation, security alignment, and — above all — AI-infrastructure and HPC capability tied to the data-center boom, the university, and NWSC rather than chasing small agency price deals.

powerStructure.name: Mark Gordon
powerStructure.title: Governor
powerStructure.note: Drives the 'Winning the AI Race' data-center agenda and chairs the Wyoming Stable Token Commission.

powerStructure.name: Jeff Clines
powerStructure.title: State CIO & ETS Director
powerStructure.note: Appointed 2024; focused on process maturity, trust, and cybersecurity for a lean central IT org.

powerStructure.name: Anthony Apollo
powerStructure.title: Executive Director
powerStructure.note: Leads FRNT (Frontier Stable Token), the first U.S. publicly-issued stable token — face of the state's digital-asset program.

powerStructure.name: Wyoming Legislature — Joint Appropriations Committee
powerStructure.title: Appropriations & fiscal
powerStructure.note: Sets the biennial budget and ETS rate authority; the 2025 supplemental did not pass, tightening agency budgets.

news.title: Crusoe pauses 'Project Jade' Cheyenne campus 'at the request of our customer'
news.impact: Wyoming's flagship multi-GW AI data center is on hold — near-term uncertainty for the state's largest data-center bet; qualify Jade-dependent pipeline.

news.title: Federal court enjoins NSF's NWSC operations transition after UCAR lawsuit
news.impact: Derecho/NWSC governance shake-up legally stalled — status quo (UCAR-managed) holds; HPC refresh path remains uncertain.

news.title: Microsoft announces intent to expand Cheyenne datacenters (~3,200 acres)
news.impact: Signals a multiyear hyperscale buildout and deepening AI-infrastructure ecosystem around Cheyenne.

news.title: Wyoming pitches data-center developers at closed-door Jackson summit
news.impact: Google, Microsoft, Meta and AWS attend; the state is actively competing for AI capacity.

news.title: Wyoming publicly launches the Frontier Stable Token (FRNT)
news.impact: First U.S. publicly-issued, fully-reserved stable token goes live for public purchase, cementing digital-asset leadership.

news.title: Gov. Gordon proposes ~$11.13B 2027-28 budget
news.impact: Sets the next biennial spending frame ($3.35B GF/BRA, ~$3.74B ongoing GF revenue; LSRA rainy day ~$1.38B per NASBO); investment income keeps the state cautious but solvent.

news.title: Crusoe/Tallgrass 'Project Jade' 1.8GW AI data center unveiled for Cheyenne
news.impact: Anchor AI campus — power use could dwarf the state's residential load; first buildings expected ~2027.

dellActions: Lead with AI-infrastructure: tie Dell ISG / AI Factory offerings to the Cheyenne data-center boom (Crusoe, Meta, Microsoft) and pursue ecosystem and supply pull-through around those campuses.
Engage the University of Wyoming and NWSC as HPC/research-computing accounts on GPU and infrastructure refresh — but qualify NWSC's NSF transition status first.
Position cybersecurity, consolidation, and lifecycle modernization to ETS that map directly to its 2026-2028 governance and risk-management themes for a lean central IT org.
Validate Dell's NASPO ValuePoint and A&I statewide-contract coverage so executive-branch agencies can buy without a custom solicitation.
Use the 'digital sovereignty on Wyoming power' narrative (Gordon's AI-race agenda, the FRNT stable token) to frame sovereign/private AI infrastructure for state and partner workloads.
